message:
feat(pager): extend ARC cache with scan-resistant admission and ghost list tracking

Expand the ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache) implementation with:
- Scan-resistant admission filtering to prevent one-shot sequential
  access patterns from evicting frequently-used pages
- Ghost list tracking for recently-evicted entries, enabling the ARC
  adaptation heuristic to measure recency vs frequency pressure more
  accurately
- Additional cache statistics and introspection methods

Update .beads/issues.jsonl with current issue tracker state.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

### Summary
Added 24 tests + 2 proptest suites covering SSI serialization anomaly detection,
latch-free concurrent stress, EBR version reclamation liveness, rebase correctness,
and CAS version install with ABA protection.

### Changes

**`crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/ssi_anomaly_tests.rs`** (NEW)
- 24 tests + 2 proptest suites across 5 categories:

**§1 Serialization Anomaly Detection (7 tests):**
- `ssi_anomaly_write_skew_detected`: Classic T1 reads A writes B, T2 reads B writes A
- `ssi_anomaly_phantom_insert_detected`: Range scan + insert via MockActiveTxn
- `ssi_anomaly_phantom_via_registry`: Phantom through full ConcurrentRegistry path
- `ssi_anomaly_lost_update_prevented_by_fcw`: FCW prevents second write to same page
- `ssi_anomaly_three_way_write_skew_cycle`: 3-txn cycle, at least one aborts
- `ssi_anomaly_committed_pivot_abort`: CommittedReaderInfo with had_in_rw forces abort
- `ssi_anomaly_marked_for_abort_propagation`: marked_for_abort flag → MarkedForAbort

**§2 Latch-Free Stress (2 tests):**
- `latch_free_stress_64_threads_hot_pages`: 64 threads, 8 hot pages, 500 ops each
- `ssi_validation_stress_16_concurrent_pairs`: 16 thread pairs, write-skew detection

**§3 EBR Liveness (4 tests):**
- `ebr_guard_lifecycle_metrics`: Pin/unpin 5 guards, verify counts
- `ebr_deferred_retirement_and_flush`: 10 retirements, 1 flush
- `ebr_stale_reader_detection`: 1ms threshold, sleep 5ms, detect stale
- `ebr_concurrent_guard_pinning`: 16 threads × 50 guards, all unpinned after

**§4 Rebase Correctness (4 tests):**
- `rebase_fcw_conflict_detected`: FCW on page committed between snapshot and commit
- `rebase_disjoint_pages_both_commit`: Non-overlapping pages succeed
- `rebase_abort_releases_locks`: Abort frees locks for subsequent txns
- `rebase_sequential_abort_retry_cycles`: 10 cycles of commit/abort on same page

**§5 CAS Version Install (3 tests):**
- `cas_version_arena_aba_protection`: 100 alloc, free 50, verify generation mismatch
- `cas_version_arena_double_free_panics`: should_panic on generation mismatch
- `cas_commit_index_concurrent_monotonic`: 16 threads × 500 updates, monotonic

**§PROPTEST (2 suites):**
- `prop_ssi_validation_never_panics`: 500 random scenarios
- `prop_ssi_disjoint_sets_always_commit`: Disjoint read/write sets always commit

**`crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/lib.rs`**
- Added `#[cfg(test)] mod ssi_anomaly_tests;`

### Test Results
- 901 MVCC tests passing (24 new + 877 existing)
- All quality gates green: cargo check, clippy (pedantic+nursery), fmt

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:09Z"},{"id":2682,"issue_id":"bd-6d9v","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Benchmark — large transaction (100K/1M row single-txn insert)

### What
Measure performance of very large transactions that stress the WAL and page cache.

### Benchmark Design
- Single transaction: BEGIN, INSERT N rows, COMMIT
- N = 100,000 and 1,000,000
- Table with 5 columns: id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT, score REAL, created TEXT
- Row size ~200 bytes → 100K rows ≈ 20MB, 1M rows ≈ 200MB

### Why This Matters
Large transactions exercise:
- WAL growth (C SQLite: WAL file grows until checkpoint)
- MVCC version chain (FrankenSQLite: page versions accumulate)
- Memory pressure (page cache turnover)
- Commit latency (fsync of large WAL vs MVCC commit)

### Metrics
- Total time for INSERT phase
- Commit latency (time from last INSERT to COMMIT completion)
- Peak memory usage during transaction
- WAL file size at commit time (C SQLite) / version chain depth (FrankenSQLite)
- Post-VACUUM database size (should be identical)

### Expected Behavior
- FrankenSQLite may have advantages in commit speed (no WAL checkpoint needed)
- C SQLite may have advantages in insert speed (mature, optimized C code)
- Memory characteristics will differ due to MVCC vs WAL approach

### Acceptance Criteria
- Benchmarks for both 100K and 1M row transactions
- Total time, commit latency, and memory reported
- Both backends produce same final state (logical comparison)
- No OOM or crash on 1M row transaction

 {"id":"bd-6i2s","title":"§14 Newer Extension Features: json_pretty + FTS5 secure-delete + Geopoly","description":"E2E Test:
- test_e2e_bd_6i2s_compliance: concrete E2E identifier for structure lint compliance.

## SUMMARY
Implement newer extension features spanning multiple extension crates that were added in recent SQLite versions: json_pretty (3.46+ in JSON1 -- pretty-printing JSON with configurable indentation), FTS5 secure-delete (3.44+ -- physically removing deleted content from the FTS5 index rather than just marking as deleted), and Geopoly extension functions built on R*-Tree (polygon operations for spatial data). This bead covers the cross-cutting integration concerns: ensuring these newer features are available when their parent extensions are enabled, that they follow the same feature-gating patterns as their parent crates, and that version-detection queries correctly report support for these features. Also covers any extension features added in SQLite 3.43+ through 3.52.0 that enhance existing extensions.

## KEY DATA STRUCTURES / ALGORITHMS
- json_pretty(X [, INDENT]): takes JSON text or JSONB and returns pretty-printed text with newlines and indentation. Default INDENT is 4 spaces. Custom indent string (e.g., tab character via char(9)) supported. Must handle all JSON types (objects, arrays, nested structures).
- FTS5 secure-delete: enabled via INSERT INTO fts_table(fts_table) VALUES('secure-delete=1'). When enabled, DELETE operations physically remove term occurrences from segment pages rather than leaving tombstone markers. This prevents deleted content from appearing in integrity-check output or being recoverable from the database file. Requires segment page rewriting during delete.
- Geopoly: built on R*-Tree, provides polygon operations. Functions: geopoly_overlap(P1,P2), geopoly_within(P1,P2), geopoly_area(P), geopoly_blob(P), geopoly_json(P), geopoly_svg(P), geopoly_bbox(P), geopoly_contains_point(P,X,Y), geopoly_group_bbox(P), geopoly_regular(X,Y,R,N), geopoly_ccw(P), geopoly_xform(P,A,B,C,D,E,F). Polygon format: 4-byte header + pairs of 32-bit float coordinates.
- FTS5 contentless-delete (3.43+): content='' with contentless_delete=1 enables DELETE on contentless FTS5 tables using tombstone markers in the index.
- JSON1 JSONB (3.45+): binary JSON format and all jsonb_* function variants.
- json_valid FLAGS parameter (3.45+): bitmask controlling which formats to validate.

## NORMATIVE INVARIANTS
1. json_pretty defaults to 4-space indentation; custom indent via string parameter.
2. json_pretty of already-pretty JSON re-formats it (normalize indentation).
3. FTS5 secure-delete physically removes content; after secure-delete, deleted terms are not recoverable from the file.
4. FTS5 secure-delete is a per-table setting, not global.
5. Geopoly functions are available when the R*-Tree extension is enabled.
6. Contentless-delete (3.43+) is a separate mode from regular contentless.
7. All newer features follow the feature-gating pattern of their parent extension crate.
8. Version-detection queries (sqlite_compileoption_used) correctly report support for these features.

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS
1. test_json_pretty_object: json_pretty('{"a":1,"b":[2,3]}') produces formatted output with newlines and 4-space indent
2. test_json_pretty_custom_indent: json_pretty('{"a":1}', '\t') uses tab indentation
3. test_json_pretty_nested: deeply nested JSON is correctly indented
4. test_json_pretty_array: json_pretty('[1,2,3]') formats array elements on separate lines
5. test_json_pretty_idempotent: json_pretty(json_pretty(X)) normalizes indentation
6. test_fts5_secure_delete_enable: INSERT INTO fts(fts) VALUES('secure-delete=1') succeeds
7. test_fts5_secure_delete_removes: after DELETE with secure-delete=1, deleted terms are physically removed
8. test_fts5_secure_delete_integrity: integrity-check passes after secure-delete operations
9. test_fts5_secure_delete_not_recoverable: deleted content is not recoverable from file bytes
10. test_fts5_contentless_delete: contentless_delete=1 enables DELETE on contentless tables
11. test_fts5_contentless_delete_tombstone: deleted entries no longer match queries
12. test_geopoly_overlap_true: overlapping polygons detected
13. test_geopoly_overlap_false: non-overlapping polygons correctly reported
14. test_geopoly_within_contained: polygon fully within another detected
15. test_geopoly_within_not_contained: non-contained polygon correctly reported
16. test_geopoly_area_triangle: area of triangle computed correctly
17. test_geopoly_area_square: area of unit square = 1.0
18. test_geopoly_contains_point_inside: point inside polygon returns true
19. test_geopoly_contains_point_outside: point outside polygon returns false
20. test_geopoly_json_roundtrip: geopoly_json(geopoly_blob(geojson)) = normalized geojson
21. test_geopoly_svg_output: geopoly_svg produces valid SVG path string
22. test_geopoly_regular_hexagon: geopoly_regular(0,0,1,6) creates 6-vertex polygon
23. test_geopoly_ccw_winding: geopoly_ccw ensures counter-clockwise vertex order
24. test_geopoly_xform_translate: affine translation moves polygon correctly
25. test_geopoly_group_bbox_aggregate: aggregate bounding box encloses all input polygons
26. test_jsonb_functions_available: jsonb_set, jsonb_array, etc. are callable
27. test_json_valid_flags: json_valid with FLAGS parameter works (3.45+)
28. test_newer_features_version_detect: sqlite_compileoption_used reports correct values

- test_e2e_bd_6i2s_compliance: concrete E2E identifier for structure lint compliance.
Create databases exercising all newer extension features together. Test json_pretty with various JSON structures and indentation options. Test FTS5 secure-delete by inserting/deleting documents and verifying deleted content is not in the file. Test Geopoly with polygon intersection/containment/area queries on geographic-like data. Test contentless-delete mode. Verify all features work correctly when parent extensions are enabled and are absent when feature-gated off. Compare all results against C sqlite3.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. json_pretty produces correctly formatted output with configurable indentation.
2. FTS5 secure-delete physically removes deleted content from index segments.
3. FTS5 contentless-delete enables DELETE on contentless tables.
4. All Geopoly functions produce correct results for polygon operations.
5. JSONB format and all jsonb_* variants work correctly.
6. json_valid FLAGS parameter controls validation strictness.
7. Newer features follow parent extension feature-gating patterns.
8. All results match C sqlite3 for these newer features.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:48:09.483460565Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T00:26:26.740728017Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T00:26:26.740702238Z","close_reason":"Validated newer extension features implementation/compliance: cargo test -p fsqlite-ext-fts5 -p fsqlite-ext-rtree and cargo test -p fsqlite-harness --test bd_6i2s_newer_extension_features_compliance all passing","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-6i2s","depends_on_id":"bd-2hor","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-6i2s","depends_on_id":"bd-3c7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2683,"issue_id":"bd-6i2s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# §14 Newer Extension Features: json_pretty + FTS5 secure-delete + Geopoly

## Scope

This bead covers the newer extension features that were added in recent SQLite versions (3.43-3.46+) and need explicit implementation attention, as well as extension scope coverage for Geopoly, JSONB, dbpage, csv, and decimal.

## Spec References

- §14.1.1: "json_pretty(X [, INDENT]) -> text (SQLite 3.46+). Returns a pretty-printed version of JSON text X. INDENT defaults to 4 spaces"
- §14.1.4: "JSONB is a binary encoding of JSON stored as a BLOB... Node types (lower 4 bits): null(0x0), true(0x1), false(0x2), int(0x3), int5(0x4), float(0x5), float5(0x6), text(0x7), textj(0x8), text5(0x9), textraw(0xA), array(0xB), object(0xC)"
- §14.2.6: "Contentless-delete (SQLite 3.43+): content='' with contentless_delete=1. Supports DELETE operations, maintaining delete-marker tombstone"
- §14.2.7: "secure-delete (SQLite 3.44+): causes DELETE operations to physically remove content from the index (not just mark as deleted)"
- §14.4: Geopoly extension: geopoly_overlap, geopoly_within, geopoly_area, geopoly_contains_point, etc.
- §14.7: dbpage virtual table (read/write access to database pages), csv virtual table, decimal extension (arbitrary-precision)

## Requirements

### json_pretty (3.46+)
1. Implement json_pretty(X) returning pretty-printed JSON with 4-space default indentation
2. Implement json_pretty(X, INDENT) with custom indentation string
3. Error on invalid JSON input (consistent with json() behavior)
4. JSONB input should be converted to pretty text output

### JSONB Binary Format
5. Implement the JSONB node-type encoding: 4-bit type + 4-bit size-of-payload-size header byte
6. Support all 13 node types: null(0x0) through object(0xC)
7. jsonb() converts text JSON to JSONB blob; json() converts JSONB to text
8. All jsonb_* variants (jsonb_extract, jsonb_set, etc.) MUST produce correct JSONB output

### FTS5 Contentless-Delete (3.43+)
9. Support `contentless_delete=1` option on contentless FTS5 tables
10. DELETE operations insert tombstone markers in the inverted index
11. Deleted documents MUST NOT appear in search results

### FTS5 secure-delete (3.44+)
12. Support `INSERT INTO t(t) VALUES('secure-delete=1')` configuration command
13. When enabled, DELETE physically removes content from index segments
14. After secure-delete, integrity-check must not find traces of deleted content
15. Deleted content must not be recoverable from the database file

### Geopoly Extension
16. Implement core Geopoly functions: geopoly_overlap, geopoly_within, geopoly_area, geopoly_contains_point
17. Polygon storage format: 4-byte header + pairs of 32-bit float coordinates
18. geopoly_json/geopoly_blob conversion between GeoJSON and internal format
19. geopoly_regular(X, Y, R, N) for generating regular N-gons

### Miscellaneous Extensions
20. dbpage virtual table: SELECT/UPDATE access to raw database pages
21. csv virtual table: read CSV files as virtual tables with header/columns options
22. decimal extension: arbitrary-precision decimal_add, decimal_sub, decimal_mul, decimal_sum, decimal_cmp

## Unit Test Specifications

### Test 1: `test_json_pretty_default_indent`
Verify json_pretty('{"a":1,"b":[2,3]}') produces multi-line output with 4-space indentation. Verify nested objects/arrays are properly indented.

### Test 2: `test_json_pretty_custom_indent`
Verify json_pretty('{"a":1}', char(9)) uses tab indentation. Verify json_pretty('{"a":1}', '..') uses '..' as indent.

### Test 3: `test_jsonb_roundtrip`
Convert text JSON to JSONB via jsonb(). Convert back via json(). Verify semantic equality. Verify JSONB blob is smaller than text. Verify all 13 node types can be encoded/decoded.

### Test 4: `test_jsonb_node_type_encoding`
Manually construct JSONB blobs for each node type (null, true, false, int, float, text, array, object) and verify json() decodes them correctly. Verify header byte has correct 4-bit type in lower nibble.

### Test 5: `test_fts5_contentless_delete`
Create contentless FTS5 table with contentless_delete=1. Insert documents. Delete one. Search for terms from deleted document. Verify zero results.

### Test 6: `test_fts5_secure_delete`
Create FTS5 table. Insert documents. Enable secure-delete. Delete a document. Run integrity-check. Read raw database pages via dbpage and verify the deleted document's text is not present in any page.

### Test 7: `test_geopoly_contains_point`
Create a square polygon (0,0)-(10,0)-(10,10)-(0,10). Verify geopoly_contains_point returns true for (5,5) and false for (15,15). Verify geopoly_area returns 100.0.

### Test 8: `test_geopoly_overlap_within`
Create two overlapping rectangles. Verify geopoly_overlap returns 1. Create a small rectangle entirely inside a large one. Verify geopoly_within returns 1 for the small one.

### Test 9: `test_decimal_arbitrary_precision`
Verify decimal_add('0.1', '0.2') = '0.3' (exact, no floating-point error). Verify decimal_mul('999999999999999999', '2') = '1999999999999999998'. Verify decimal_cmp('0.1', '0.10') = 0.

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:10Z"},{"id":2688,"issue_id":"bd-72im","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Benchmark — read-heavy SELECT workload

### What
Measure query performance for various SELECT patterns on real-world databases.

### Benchmark Queries (run against golden copy data)
1. **Point lookup**: `SELECT * FROM issues WHERE id = ?` (B-tree seek)
2. **Range scan**: `SELECT * FROM issues WHERE created_at > ? AND created_at < ?`
3. **Full table scan**: `SELECT count(*) FROM issues`
4. **JOIN**: `SELECT i.*, c.body FROM issues i JOIN comments c ON i.id = c.issue_id` (if schema supports)
5. **Aggregate**: `SELECT status, count(*) FROM issues GROUP BY status`
6. **Subquery**: `SELECT * FROM issues WHERE id IN (SELECT issue_id FROM comments)`
7. **ORDER BY + LIMIT**: `SELECT * FROM issues ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10`

### Real Data Advantage
Using actual beads.db databases means we're benchmarking against real-world data distributions, not synthetic uniform data. This gives more realistic query planner and cache behavior.

### Benchmark Configuration
- Each query run 1000 times (warm cache)
- Also benchmark cold cache (fresh database open per iteration)
- Report: mean, median, p95, p99 latency

### Expected Results
- Read performance should be comparable between backends (both use B-tree)
- FrankenSQLite may show different characteristics due to MVCC overhead on reads (version chain traversal)
- Any significant read regression should be investigated

### Acceptance Criteria
- At least 5 SELECT patterns benchmarked
- Results for both warm and cold cache
- Latency distribution (not just mean)
- Run against at least 3 different golden databases (varying sizes)
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:43Z"},{"id":2689,"issue_id":"bd-72im","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## REVISION NOTE (IvoryWaterfall audit)

### Issue: JOINs may not be fully available
Some queries reference JOINs (e.g., `SELECT i.*, c.body FROM issues i JOIN comments c ON ...`), but FrankenSQLite's JOIN support may not be complete enough for all patterns. For the read benchmark:

**Recommended approach**: Start with queries that work TODAY (point lookups, full scans, aggregates, ORDER BY + LIMIT), then add JOIN benchmarks once the JOIN codegen is verified.

**Fallback queries** if JOINs aren't ready:
- `SELECT * FROM issues WHERE status = ?` (indexed lookup)
- `SELECT count(*), status FROM issues GROUP BY status` (aggregate)
- `SELECT * FROM issues ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10` (sort + limit)
- `SELECT * FROM issues WHERE title LIKE '%search_term%'` (full scan with filter)

### Issue: Must use benchmark fairness protocol
All benchmarks in this bead MUST apply the PRAGMA settings defined in bd-3qeq (Benchmark Fairness Protocol) before running.

### Testing Addition
Each query benchmark should also verify result correctness (not just timing):
- Run query on both backends
- Compare result counts
- Compare first/last rows
This catches correctness regressions that only manifest in benchmark mode.

 {"id":"bd-t6sv2.5","title":"Transaction Lifecycle Analyzer & Advisor","description":"**Idea #5 - Phase 2**

Provide deep visibility into transaction behavior with actionable guidance. Transaction lifecycle data feeds directly into GC tuning (bd-t6sv2.15) and checkpoint scheduling (bd-t6sv2.7).

**Components:**
1. **TxnTracer**
   - Track: begin_time, first_read, first_write, commit/rollback_time
   - Page access pattern (read set size, write set size, evolution over time)
   - Savepoint depth and rollback frequency
   - Snapshot age for MVCC pressure assessment
   - Build on existing transaction instrumentation in `lifecycle.rs` (159KB, 29 tracing spans)

2. **TxnMetrics**
   - PRAGMA fsqlite_txn_stats: active_count, avg_duration_ms, long_running_count, max_snapshot_age_ms
   - Per-transaction state via virtual table: SELECT txn_id, duration_ms, pages_read, pages_written, snapshot_age_ms FROM fsqlite_transactions
   - GC pressure indicator: oldest_active_snapshot vs current commit_seq (feeds bd-t6sv2.15)

3. **TxnAdvisor**
   - Warn: "Transaction held for >N seconds without activity" (configurable threshold via PRAGMA)
   - Suggest: "Consider batching these M small transactions" (detect rapid begin/commit cycles)
   - Alert: "Snapshot too old, blocking GC - consider shorter transaction scope"
   - Alert: "Transaction read set growing beyond N pages - consider narrower queries"

4. **TxnVisualizer**
   - Timeline data export as JSON (txn start/end, conflict points, page accesses)
   - Conflict points annotated with resolution outcome
   - Export format compatible with external timeline tools (Chrome DevTools trace format)
   - Uses shared observability infrastructure from bd-t6sv2.1

**Implementation:**
- Extend Transaction struct in `lifecycle.rs` with lightweight instrumentation
- New virtual table: fsqlite_transactions (read-only, reflects in-memory state)
- Advisory system with configurable PRAGMA thresholds
- Build on shared observability crate from bd-t6sv2.1

**Files to Modify:**
- crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/lifecycle.rs (transaction instrumentation)
- crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/core_types.rs (snapshot age tracking)
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/pragma.rs (advisory thresholds, stats PRAGMAs)
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs (virtual table registration)
- crates/fsqlite-observability/ (shared infrastructure from bd-t6sv2.1)

**CRITICAL: MVCC Preservation**
Transaction tracing MUST NOT alter transaction ordering or introduce synchronization. Instrumentation uses atomic counters and lock-free data structures. The virtual table provides a read-only snapshot of transaction state - it MUST NOT hold any locks while returning results.

**Test Requirements:**
- Unit: timing accuracy under controlled delays
  - Log: txn_id, begin_ts_ns, first_read_ts_ns, first_write_ts_ns, commit_ts_ns, duration_ms

  - Verify: duration = commit_ts - begin_ts within 1ms tolerance
- Unit: page access tracking correctness
  - Log: txn_id, pages_read, pages_written, read_set_pages, write_set_pages
  - Verify: read_set superset of write_set (every written page was also read)
  - Verify: page counts match actual B-tree operations
- Unit: advisor trigger thresholds
  - Log: advisor_type (long_txn|batch_suggest|gc_pressure|large_read_set), txn_id, threshold, actual_value
  - Verify: advisor fires at exactly the configured threshold
  - Verify: no advisories when all transactions are healthy
- Integration: multi-txn scenario with correct lifecycle tracking
  - Log: txn_count, concurrent_peak, avg_duration_ms, max_snapshot_age_ms
  - Scenario: 10 concurrent transactions, verify all lifecycles tracked independently
  - Scenario: nested savepoints, verify savepoint depth tracked correctly
- E2E: virtual table returns accurate data under concurrent load
  - Log: query_time_ms, rows_returned, active_txn_count, gc_pressure_score
  - Verify: virtual table query does not block concurrent transactions
  - Verify: data consistent (no torn reads of in-flight transaction state)
- E2E: advisor recommendations verified against actual performance
  - Log: advisory, action_taken, before_metric, after_metric, improvement_pct
  - Verify: following advisor recommendations actually improves the metric

**Verification Script:**

3. Verifies lifecycle timing matches expected patterns
4. Triggers each advisory type and verifies output
5. Outputs pass/fail with evidence JSON

**Success Criteria:**

- Anti-patterns detected and explained with actionable guidance
- GC-blocking snapshots identified early (before memory pressure)

 {"id":"bd-t6sv2.6","title":"Query Plan Concurrency Hints","description":"**Idea #6 - Phase 2**

Extend EXPLAIN output to show concurrent writer implications, enabling developers to predict and prevent conflicts at query plan time.

**Components:**
1. **LockPrediction**
   - Static analysis: which tables/pages likely accessed by each query
   - Worst-case lock footprint estimation (based on table statistics)
   - Index vs table scan lock patterns (index scan = fewer page locks)
   - Annotate with page count estimates from planner statistics

2. **ConflictProbability**
   - Based on table statistics, access patterns, and conflict history from bd-t6sv2.1
   - "Low/Medium/High conflict risk" annotation per table access
   - Hotspot identification in plan output (pages with historical conflict density)

3. **ConcurrencyAdvisor**
   - Suggest index additions to reduce lock scope (fewer pages touched = fewer conflicts)
   - Recommend transaction batching strategies based on access patterns
   - Identify table scans that could cause broad page locking
   - Suggest covering indexes to avoid table page access entirely

4. **EXPLAIN CONCURRENT**
   - New EXPLAIN variant showing concurrency analysis alongside query plan
   - JSON output for tooling integration
   - Diff mode: compare two queries for potential conflict overlap
   - Show: estimated_pages_locked, conflict_risk_per_table, suggested_indexes

**Implementation:**
- Extend CodegenContext in `fsqlite-vdbe` with lock analysis metadata
- New EXPLAIN handler in `fsqlite-core/src/pragma.rs`
- Query plan annotation framework in `fsqlite-planner`
- Consume conflict statistics from bd-t6sv2.1 for historical risk

**Files to Modify:**
- crates/fsqlite-planner/src/ (lock footprint estimation during planning)
- crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/ (EXPLAIN CONCURRENT output generation)
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/pragma.rs (EXPLAIN CONCURRENT handler)
- crates/fsqlite-observability/ (consume conflict stats from bd-t6sv2.1)

**CRITICAL: MVCC Preservation**
Lock prediction is purely advisory and read-only. It MUST NOT alter query execution, introduce additional locks, or change transaction behavior. The EXPLAIN CONCURRENT output is informational only.

**Test Requirements:**
- Unit: lock prediction accuracy for known query patterns
  - Log: query_sql, predicted_pages, predicted_tables, conflict_risk_level
  - Verify: table scan predicts higher page count than index scan
  - Verify: covering index scan predicts zero table page accesses
  - Verify: same query produces deterministic predictions (plan stability)
- Unit: conflict probability calculation
  - Log: table_name, historical_conflicts, access_pattern, risk_level (low|medium|high), confidence
  - Verify: risk level correlates with actual conflict rates from test workloads
- Integration: EXPLAIN CONCURRENT output format correctness
  - Log: explain_rows, json_valid, fields_present, annotation_count
  - Verify: JSON output parseable and contains all required fields
  - Verify: text output human-readable and aligned
- Integration: advisor recommendations reduce actual conflicts
  - Log: query_before, suggestion, query_after, conflicts_before, conflicts_after
  - Scenario: table scan query -> advisor suggests index -> verify fewer page locks
  - Scenario: wide row table -> advisor suggests narrower access pattern
- E2E: full EXPLAIN CONCURRENT workflow with concurrent writers
  - Log: query_count, total_predictions, prediction_accuracy_pct
  - Verify: predictions correlate with actual runtime conflict behavior
  - Verify: diff mode correctly identifies overlapping page access between two queries
- E2E: EXPLAIN CONCURRENT does not affect query performance
  - Log: query_time_without_explain_ms, query_time_with_explain_ms, overhead_pct
  - Verify: overhead < 5% for EXPLAIN CONCURRENT vs regular EXPLAIN

**Verification Script:**
Create scripts/verify_explain_concurrent.sh that:
1. Runs EXPLAIN CONCURRENT on a set of known queries
2. Compares predictions against actual runtime conflict behavior
3. Verifies advisor suggestions produce measurable improvement
4. Validates JSON output format
5. Outputs pass/fail with accuracy statistics

**Success Criteria:**
- Developers predict conflicts before runtime with reasonable accuracy
- Query optimization considers concurrency impact
- Anti-patterns caught at plan time with actionable suggestions
- EXPLAIN CONCURRENT output integrates with external query analysis tools

 {"id":"bd-t6sv2.7","title":"Checkpoint Scheduling Advisor","description":"**Idea #7 - Phase 2**

Intelligent checkpoint scheduling based on workload analysis. Checkpoint timing directly impacts write amplification (bd-t6sv2.9) and is informed by transaction lifecycle data (bd-t6sv2.5).

**Components:**
1. **WALMonitor**
   - Track WAL size over time (pages, bytes)
   - Write rate estimation (pages/second, rolling window)
   - Checkpoint duration history (with variance tracking)
   - Build on existing checkpoint infrastructure in `checkpoint_executor.rs` (22KB)

2. **CheckpointPredictor**
   - Estimate time until WAL threshold reached
   - Predict checkpoint impact on writers (based on active transaction count from bd-t6sv2.5)
   - Model write stall probability (based on WAL growth rate vs checkpoint throughput)

3. **SchedulingAdvisor**
   - Recommend checkpoint timing: "Checkpoint now: low write activity detected"
   - Warn: "WAL growth rate exceeds checkpoint throughput - consider TRUNCATE mode"
   - Suggest: "Optimal checkpoint window: low-activity period detected at [time range]"
   - Alert: "WAL size exceeds N pages - checkpoint urgently needed"

4. **AutoCheckpoint Enhancement**
   - Adaptive auto_checkpoint threshold based on workload patterns
   - Write-rate-aware scheduling (delay checkpoint during write bursts)
   - Low-activity window detection (checkpoint during quiet periods)
   - PRAGMA fsqlite_checkpoint_schedule for manual override

**Implementation:**
- Extend `crates/fsqlite-wal/src/checkpoint_executor.rs` with metrics collection
- Add workload analysis to `crates/fsqlite-wal/src/wal.rs`
- New PRAGMAs for checkpoint scheduling in `fsqlite-core/src/pragma.rs`
- Consume transaction lifecycle data from bd-t6sv2.5

**Files to Modify:**
- crates/fsqlite-wal/src/checkpoint_executor.rs (metrics, adaptive scheduling)
- crates/fsqlite-wal/src/checkpoint.rs (checkpoint mode analysis)
- crates/fsqlite-wal/src/wal.rs (WAL size tracking, write rate estimation)
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/pragma.rs (new PRAGMAs)
- crates/fsqlite-observability/ (shared infrastructure)

**CRITICAL: MVCC Preservation**
Checkpoint scheduling MUST NOT block concurrent writers unnecessarily. Adaptive scheduling may DELAY checkpoints (not block writers). The auto_checkpoint threshold adjustment must preserve the property that writers are never blocked waiting for a checkpoint to start.

**Test Requirements:**
- Unit: WAL size tracking accuracy
  - Log: wal_pages, wal_bytes, writes_since_last_checkpoint, growth_rate_pages_per_sec
  - Verify: tracked size matches actual WAL file size
  - Verify: growth rate calculation stable over rolling window
- Unit: checkpoint duration prediction accuracy
  - Log: predicted_duration_ms, actual_duration_ms, prediction_error_pct, wal_pages
  - Verify: prediction within 20% of actual for steady-state workloads
- Unit: advisor threshold triggers
  - Log: advisor_type (checkpoint_now|wal_growth_warning|optimal_window|urgent), threshold, actual_value
  - Verify: each advisor type fires at exactly the configured threshold
  - Verify: no advisories when WAL growth is within normal bounds
- Integration: adaptive scheduling reduces write stalls
  - Log: stall_count_baseline, stall_count_adaptive, improvement_pct, checkpoint_count
  - Scenario: bursty write workload with baseline vs adaptive scheduling
  - Verify: adaptive scheduling produces fewer write stalls
- Integration: checkpoint scheduling respects active transactions
  - Log: active_txn_count, checkpoint_delayed, delay_reason, eventual_checkpoint_time_ms
  - Verify: checkpoint delayed when many active transactions
  - Verify: checkpoint eventually completes (no indefinite delay)
- E2E: full adaptive scheduling under realistic workload
  - Log: total_writes, total_checkpoints, avg_wal_size_pages, max_wal_size_pages, stall_rate_pct
  - Verify: WAL size bounded without manual tuning
  - Verify: checkpoint timing correlates with low-activity periods
- E2E: PRAGMA override correctly controls scheduling
  - Log: pragma_value, scheduled_checkpoint_time_ms, actual_checkpoint_time_ms
  - Verify: manual PRAGMA overrides adaptive scheduling

**Verification Script:**
Create scripts/verify_checkpoint_scheduling.sh that:
1. Runs a bursty write workload with default checkpoint settings
2. Runs the same workload with adaptive checkpoint scheduling
3. Compares stall counts and WAL growth patterns
4. Verifies advisor recommendations match workload characteristics
5. Outputs pass/fail with comparative statistics

**Success Criteria:**
- Checkpoint never surprises high-write workloads with unexpected stalls
- WAL growth bounded without manual tuning in common workloads
- Operators understand checkpoint behavior and can override when needed
- Write stall rate measurably reduced by adaptive scheduling

-{"id":"bd-t6sv2.8","title":"Page Cache Efficiency Monitor","description":"**Idea #8 - Phase 2**

Expose page cache behavior for tuning and debugging. The ARC cache implementation lives in `fsqlite-pager/src/arc_cache.rs` (113KB) and is critical for MVCC performance since it keys on `(PageNumber, TxnId)` for version coexistence.

**Components:**
1. **CacheMetrics**
   - Hit rate (overall, per-table estimated via page-to-table mapping)
   - Eviction frequency and patterns (T1 vs T2 evictions)
   - Dirty page ratio over time
   - MVCC version coexistence overhead (pages with multiple versions cached)

2. **ARCAnalyzer**
   - ARC ghost list (B1/B2) effectiveness: ghost hit rate indicates ARC adaptation quality
   - T1/T2 balance: target parameter `p` tracking over time

 {"id":"bd-t6sv2.9","title":"Write Amplification Tracker","description":"**Idea #9 - Phase 2**

Measure and report write amplification for storage efficiency. Write amplification is directly impacted by checkpoint scheduling (bd-t6sv2.7) and page cache behavior (bd-t6sv2.8).

**Components:**
1. **WriteMetrics**
   - Logical writes: user data bytes (actual row data changed)
   - Physical writes: actual I/O bytes (pages written to WAL + checkpoint writes)
   - Write amplification factor: physical/logical ratio
   - MVCC overhead: bytes written for version chain management vs user data

2. **ComponentBreakdown**
   - WAL overhead: framing headers, checksums, RaptorQ repair symbols
   - B-tree overhead: page splits, rebalancing, internal node updates
   - Checkpoint overhead: full page writes to main database file
   - MVCC overhead: version chain allocation, copy-on-write page duplication

3. **TrendAnalysis**
   - Write amplification over time (rolling window, configurable)
   - Correlation with table sizes (larger tables = more internal node updates)
   - Impact of index count (each index adds write amplification)
   - Comparison: MVCC mode vs hypothetical serial mode amplification

4. **OptimizationAdvisor**
   - Suggest page size adjustments (larger pages reduce per-page overhead fraction)
   - Recommend vacuum timing (reduce freelist fragmentation)
   - Identify high-overhead patterns (e.g., single-row updates on large pages)
   - Report: "RaptorQ repair symbols add N% write overhead (configurable via PRAGMA)"

**Implementation:**
- Instrument Pager in `pager.rs` with I/O byte counters (atomic)
- Track WAL frame sizes in `wal.rs`
- New PRAGMA fsqlite_write_amplification_stats
- Virtual table: fsqlite_write_amplification (component breakdown)
- Use shared observability infrastructure from bd-t6sv2.1

**Files to Modify:**
- crates/fsqlite-pager/src/pager.rs (I/O byte counters)
- crates/fsqlite-wal/src/wal.rs (frame size tracking)
- crates/fsqlite-wal/src/wal_fec.rs (RaptorQ overhead measurement)
- crates/fsqlite-wal/src/checkpoint_executor.rs (checkpoint write tracking)
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/pragma.rs (new PRAGMAs)
- crates/fsqlite-observability/ (shared infrastructure)

**CRITICAL: MVCC Preservation**
Write amplification tracking is purely observational. Counters use atomic operations and MUST NOT introduce any write barriers or I/O synchronization. The optimizer advisor suggests changes but never auto-applies them.

**Test Requirements:**
- Unit: counter accuracy for known write patterns
  - Log: logical_bytes_written, physical_bytes_written, amplification_factor, operation_type
  - Verify: single row INSERT -> logical = row bytes, physical = full page(s)
  - Verify: amplification_factor = physical / logical within tolerance
- Unit: component breakdown accuracy
  - Log: wal_overhead_bytes, btree_overhead_bytes, checkpoint_overhead_bytes, mvcc_overhead_bytes, raptorq_overhead_bytes
  - Verify: sum of components = total physical writes
  - Verify: each component >= 0 (no negative counters)
- Unit: trend analysis calculation
  - Log: window_size_ms, samples, amplification_trend (increasing|stable|decreasing), slope
  - Verify: trend detection correctly identifies increasing amplification
- Integration: write amplification under different workloads
  - Log: workload_type (insert_heavy|update_heavy|mixed), rows_written, amplification_factor, component_breakdown
  - Scenario: bulk INSERT -> verify relatively low amplification
  - Scenario: single-row UPDATEs on large table -> verify higher amplification
  - Scenario: multi-index table -> verify index contribution to amplification
- Integration: MVCC vs serial comparison
  - Log: mvcc_amplification, estimated_serial_amplification, mvcc_overhead_pct
  - Verify: MVCC overhead quantified accurately
- E2E: advisor recommendations verified
  - Log: recommendation, parameter_before, parameter_after, amplification_before, amplification_after
  - Verify: following page size recommendation reduces amplification
  - Verify: vacuum recommendation reduces freelist-related amplification
- E2E: monitoring overhead measurement
  - Log: throughput_without_tracking, throughput_with_tracking, overhead_pct
  - Verify: tracking overhead < 1% (atomic counter updates only)

**Verification Script:**
Create scripts/verify_write_amplification.sh that:
1. Runs known workloads and measures write amplification
2. Verifies component breakdown sums to total
3. Tests advisor recommendations
4. Compares MVCC vs serial mode amplification
5. Outputs pass/fail with detailed breakdown JSON

**Success Criteria:**
- Storage costs predictable with accurate amplification data
- Optimization opportunities identified and quantified
- SSD endurance maximized through informed tuning
- MVCC overhead transparently reported

All 10 failures trace to single root cause (IPK codegen). 13 new tests cover edge cases (NULL, 0, negative, multi-row, reorder, omit, delete+reinsert).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-tios","depends_on_id":"bd-26vi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2919,"issue_id":"bd-tios","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# PARITY-D3: Residual gap harvesting — probe IPK edge cases beyond test suite

## Context

After all tests pass and quality gates are green, manually probe edge cases that the existing
test suite may not cover. The goal is to find remaining behavioral divergences between
FrankenSQLite and C SQLite before closing the PARITY epic.

## Probe Queries

### INSERT OR REPLACE with IPK
```sql
CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val TEXT);
INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'original');
INSERT OR REPLACE INTO t VALUES (1, 'replaced');
SELECT * FROM t;  -- should be (1, 'replaced'), not (1, 'original') AND (1, 'replaced')
```

### INSERT ON CONFLICT (UPSERT) with IPK
```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x') ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET val='y';
SELECT * FROM t;  -- should be (1, 'y')
```

### Complex Expressions as IPK
```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES (20+22, 'computed');     -- id should be 42
INSERT INTO t VALUES (CAST('99' AS INTEGER), 'cast');  -- id should be 99
INSERT INTO t VALUES (ABS(-7), 'abs');        -- id should be 7
```

### Subquery as IPK
```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES ((SELECT MAX(id)+1 FROM t), 'from subquery');
```

### COALESCE / IIF as IPK
```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES (COALESCE(NULL, NULL, 99), 'coalesce');  -- id=99
INSERT INTO t VALUES (IIF(1=1, 42, 0), 'iif');               -- id=42
```

### Edge Value IPK
```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES (0, 'zero');
INSERT INTO t VALUES (-1, 'negative');
INSERT INTO t VALUES (9223372036854775807, 'max_i64');
```

### IPK with WITHOUT ROWID (separate semantics)
```sql
CREATE TABLE t2 (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val TEXT) WITHOUT ROWID;
INSERT INTO t2 VALUES (42, 'test');
SELECT * FROM t2;
```
Note: WITHOUT ROWID tables have different semantics — IPK is a real column, not a rowid alias.
This is a separate feature but worth probing.

### Multi-table IPK Interactions
```sql
CREATE TABLE a (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val TEXT);
CREATE TABLE b (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, ref_a INTEGER);
INSERT INTO a VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y');
INSERT INTO b SELECT id, id FROM a;  -- IPK from SELECT result
SELECT * FROM b;  -- should be (1,1), (2,2)
```

## Methodology

For each query:
1. Run against C SQLite (via rusqlite in a test)
2. Run against FrankenSQLite
3. Compare results byte-for-byte
4. If different, create a follow-on bead with the exact divergence

## Acceptance Criteria

- All probe queries are run against both engines
- Any divergences are documented as new beads with repro commands
- If no divergences: close with "all probes pass, no residual gaps"

## Dependencies

Blocked by D2 (bd-26vi) — all quality gates must pass first.

 {"id":"bd-u43hx","title":"Flat Combining for lock contention hot spots (§14.2)","description":"Implement Flat Combining (Hendler et al. 2010) for lock contention hot spots. Flat Combining turns contention into throughput: instead of N threads each acquiring a lock, one combiner thread collects all N requests and executes them in a batch, eliminating lock handoff overhead.

## Mathematical Foundation
Under high contention (N threads, lock hold time H), traditional locking throughput is approximately 1/H (one operation per lock hold). Flat Combining throughput is approximately N/H (batch of N operations per lock hold). The improvement is proportional to contention level.

## Application in FrankenSQLite
- WAL append (before parallel WAL is implemented)
- Buffer pool eviction queue
- SSI conflict table updates
- Any centralized data structure under high contention

## Alien CS Reference
Hendler, Incze, Shavit, Tzafrir 2010 Flat Combining and the Synchronization-Parallelism Tradeoff: original paper with formal throughput analysis and experimental results showing 2-10x improvement under high contention. Fatourou and Kallimanis 2012 Revisiting the Combining Synchronization Technique: optimized combining with reduced publication list overhead.

## Rust Implementation Guidance
- Define FlatCombiner<Op, Result> as a generic framework
- Publication list: Vec<AtomicPtr<CombinerSlot<Op, Result>>> with per-thread slots via thread_local!
- Combiner election: try_lock on a central Mutex; winner becomes combiner, losers park
- Batch execution: combiner iterates publication list, collects pending Ops, executes batch, writes Results
- Thread parking: use std::thread::park/unpark for efficient waiting (avoid spin loops)
- Slot lifecycle: allocate on first use, reuse across operations (avoid repeated allocation)
- Use Pin<Box<CombinerSlot>> to ensure stable addresses for AtomicPtr

## Logging Requirements
- Log combiner election at tracing::debug level: thread_id, batch_size
- Log batch execution at tracing::debug level: ops_count, elapsed_time
- Log contention metrics at tracing::info level periodically: avg_batch_size, combiner_switches_per_sec
- Log fallback to direct execution (if batch size = 1) at tracing::trace level

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Flat Combining implementation (generic combiner framework)
- [ ] Publication list with per-thread slots
- [ ] Combiner election and batch execution
- [ ] Benchmark: Flat Combining vs mutex under 32-thread contention
